paragraph: okay so I finally caved and went to Thornwick & Plum last Thursday and I genuinely did not expect to be as floored as I was. this place is tucked into a weird little alley off Verano Street in the Costabel district — you'd miss it if you didn't know it was there — and it's only been open since late February. chef Dessa Morland is doing this thing she calls coastal-mountain cooking, which sounds vague but in practice means you're getting smoked mussel toast with whipped lardo and juniper salt alongside braised lamb shank with pickled cloudberries. the room seats maybe 30, exposed timber ceiling, candles literally everywhere, and the vibe is cozy in a way that doesn't feel try-hard. we did the four-course menu at $89 per person and added the optional cheese cart for $18 which, yes, do it. the pacing was relaxed but never sluggish and our server Tomasz knew the menu cold. my one gripe: the bread situation was a single small roll per person, which felt stingy given how good it was. I'd go back every week if I could get a table.
restate_0: Thornwick & Plum has been making the rounds in the Costabel dining conversation since it opened in late February, and after finally getting a table last week I can confirm the enthusiasm is earned. Chef Dessa Morland describes her approach as coastal-mountain cooking, and you can taste the geography in every course — the smoked mussel toast with whipped lardo and juniper salt arrives like a bridge between two landscapes, and the braised lamb shank with pickled cloudberries makes its own kind of coherent sense. The four-course format runs $89 per head, and the optional cheese cart at $18 is the right call every time. The room fits about 30 guests under exposed timber beams with candlelight doing the heavy lifting on atmosphere, and it works without straining for effect. Server Tomasz clearly knows this menu inside out, and the pacing over the course of the evening was measured without becoming sluggish. One note: the bread service is a single roll per person, which reads as slightly stingy given its quality. Still, the experience is one of the more complete evenings you can have in this neighborhood right now.
restate_1: Just had the most unexpectedly special dinner at a tiny place called Thornwick & Plum — it's in that alley off Verano Street in Costabel, the one you'd walk past a hundred times. Dessa Morland is running the kitchen and she's calling what she does coastal-mountain cooking. Honestly that framing clicked for me by the second course. Smoked mussel toast with whipped lardo and juniper salt, then braised lamb with pickled cloudberries — it all tracks. The four-course menu is $89pp and you HAVE to do the cheese cart for $18, no debate. The room is intimate, maybe 30 seats, timber ceiling and candles everywhere in that cozy-but-not-corny way. Our server Tomasz was great. Only thing I'd flag: one small bread roll per person felt a little tight when the bread itself was genuinely that good. But I'd rebook in a heartbeat. The fact that this place hasn't blown up yet is surprising.
restate_2: At Thornwick & Plum, Chef Dessa Morland is building every dish around what she calls coastal-mountain cooking — a philosophy that might sound like marketing but delivers something tangible on the plate. The smoked mussel toast, layered with whipped lardo and juniper salt, demonstrates the concept more clearly than any description could. The braised lamb shank arrives with pickled cloudberries, a pairing that sounds improbable and lands as inevitable. The four-course menu is priced at $89, with a cheese cart offered as an optional addition at $18. The dining room holds approximately 30 covers, with exposed timber ceilings and candlelight throughout. Located in a side alley off Verano Street in the Costabel district, the restaurant has operated since late February and has already established a voice that feels fully formed rather than still in development. Service under Tomasz was knowledgeable and well-paced. A minor observation: the bread ration — a single roll per diner — does not match the generosity evident elsewhere on the menu.
restate_3: Thornwick & Plum — tucked into that alley off Verano Street in Costabel — is the kind of place that would slip past you unless someone put the address directly in your hand, and honestly that's probably by design. Chef Dessa Morland cooked for us last Thursday and the thing that stayed with me was how coherent everything felt across four courses. She's calling her approach coastal-mountain cooking and at $89 per person you're getting smoked mussel toast with whipped lardo and juniper salt and braised lamb shank with pickled cloudberries, which are dishes that know what they are. Add the cheese cart at $18. Do not skip it. The room is 30 seats max, timber and candles, atmosphere that doesn't announce itself. Our guy Tomasz held the whole service together with real composure. My only note: one small roll of bread per guest is not enough when the bread is that good. Definitely not the last time I'm going.
restate_4: Thornwick & Plum opened in the Costabel district this past February and quickly became one of the more talked-about openings in that part of the city. Tucked into an alley off Verano Street, the 30-seat dining room — all exposed timber beams and candlelight — was the setting for Chef Dessa Morland's coastal-mountain tasting menu this past Thursday. The four-course format is $89 per person, and the add-on cheese cart at $18 rounds out the evening in the best possible way. Morland is working with ingredients that speak to both shore and highland: the smoked mussel toast with whipped lardo and juniper salt is one of the more elegant bites available in the neighborhood right now, and the braised lamb shank with pickled cloudberries brings a tartness that lifts the whole dish. Service from Tomasz was assured and comfortable throughout. The one thing guests should know going in: bread service is a single small roll per person, which feels at odds with how good it actually is. Expect the reservation list to grow as word spreads.

paragraph: the line at Fuji Bun Co. on a Saturday morning is no joke — I waited 45 minutes and I would do it again without hesitation. this is a tiny counter-service spot in the Bellward food hall that opened sometime last October, run by two siblings, Kei and Nao Tsurugi, who grew up eating their grandmother's steamed bun recipes and have now spent what looks like considerable effort updating them for a new context. the signature is the black sesame and caramelized onion bao at $6, which is a single bun but a substantial one — the filling is rich and deeply savory in a way that reads almost like French onion soup inside a pillow. they also do a rotating weekly special; when I visited it was a smoked trout and pickled daikon version that I ate in about 90 seconds standing at a tall table. the space is small, maybe eight stools, and it gets crowded fast. cash or card accepted, but the card reader is slow. nothing on the menu tops $14, which for the Bellward hall is practically a deal. genuinely one of the best things I've eaten this year.
restate_0: A 45-minute wait in a food hall on a weekend morning is a strong editorial statement, and Fuji Bun Co. earns every minute of it. Siblings Kei and Nao Tsurugi opened their counter in the Bellward food hall last October, and what they've built around their grandmother's steamed bun recipes — updated but not overthought — has quickly become one of the more compelling things happening in the hall. The signature black sesame and caramelized onion bao ($6) reads, improbably, like French onion soup translated into pillowed dough: a single bun, substantial in a way that justifies the price. The weekly rotating special is worth tracking; a recent visit turned up a smoked trout and pickled daikon version that was gone in under two minutes. The counter is eight stools, card or cash accepted (though the card reader is characteristically slow for the hall), and nothing on the menu exceeds $14. In the context of the Bellward, that's genuine value. This is one of the more exciting counter-service openings in the city this year.

restate_2: Fuji Bun Co. occupies a small counter in the Bellward food hall, operated by siblings Kei and Nao Tsurugi since last October. The menu is grounded in their grandmother's steamed bun recipes — traditions that the two siblings have clearly worked to recontextualize without erasing. The flagship item, a black sesame and caramelized onion bao priced at $6, is a single bun that delivers a filling with the depth and richness of French onion soup. A weekly rotating special supplements the permanent menu; recent offerings have included a smoked trout and pickled daikon variation. Seating is limited to approximately eight stools and the counter draws a substantial weekend queue — wait times of 45 minutes are not unusual. The full menu stays under $14, making Fuji Bun Co. one of the more accessible options in the hall. Payment by cash or card is accepted, though the card reader operates slowly. Fuji Bun Co. represents the kind of focused, ingredient-honest cooking that elevates a food hall from a collection of stalls to a genuine dining destination.
restate_3: Stood in line for 45 minutes at Fuji Bun Co. in the Bellward food hall last Saturday and I would do it again tomorrow. Kei and Nao Tsurugi have been running this counter since last October — just two siblings making steamed buns from their grandmother's recipes and quietly running laps around most of the other food in that hall. The black sesame and caramelized onion bao at $6 is a single bun but it eats like a meal: rich, deeply savory, and it genuinely reminds you of French onion soup in a way you can't fully explain. The weekly special the day I went was smoked trout with pickled daikon, which I ate standing up in about 90 seconds and immediately regretted not ordering two. Nothing tops $14, eight stools, card reader is sluggish so cash is smarter. This is easily one of the top food finds I've made this year.
restate_4: Word around the Bellward food hall right now is Fuji Bun Co., the steamed bun counter that siblings Kei and Nao Tsurugi launched last October. The stall is a compact operation — eight stools, a counter, and a menu where nothing exceeds $14 — but the lines stretching through the hall on Saturday mornings suggest the city has found it. The anchor of the menu is the black sesame and caramelized onion bao, a $6 single bun whose filling carries the savory depth of a long-cooked French onion soup. A weekly special rotates in alongside the permanent offerings; a recent smoked trout and pickled daikon version drew significant attention. The Tsurugi siblings' approach honors their grandmother's technique while clearly having been developed with a contemporary palate in mind. Cash payment is encouraged — the card reader processes slowly. Expect a wait of up to 45 minutes on weekend mornings, and plan accordingly. Based on current trajectory, Fuji Bun Co. is a strong candidate for a permanent standalone home.

paragraph: Marigold Tempo opened in the old printworks building on Lasseter Row back in April and I've been putting off going because tasting menus in converted industrial spaces felt like a vibe I'd burned out on. I was wrong to wait. chef Orsolya Fekete is doing something that resists easy categorization — it's modernist in technique but the flavors are rooted in her family's Central European cooking, so you end up with things like a compressed cucumber sphere floating in a cold broth made from smoked bones and dried plum, which sounds unhinged but tastes like the best cold soup you've had. the space is beautiful: 14-meter ceilings, the original iron press still bolted to one wall, leather banquettes in a warm cognac tone. the nine-course menu is $175 per person, and wine pairing adds $95. we skipped the pairing and ordered by the glass instead, which worked fine. service is formally trained but the staff didn't feel stiff — there was genuine warmth in how they explained each dish. reservations open 30 days out and go fast; I'd set a calendar reminder.
restate_0: Marigold Tempo has been operating in the old printworks building on Lasseter Row since April, and the restaurant warrants the hype that has accumulated around it since opening. Chef Orsolya Fekete is working at the intersection of modernist technique and the Central European flavors of her family kitchen — a combination that produces dishes with a genuinely unusual register. A compressed cucumber sphere presented in a cold broth built from smoked bones and dried plum demonstrates the approach: the construction is contemporary, but the taste is ancestral, familiar in a way that is also entirely surprising. The space amplifies the experience: 14-meter ceilings, the original iron printing press anchored to the wall, leather banquettes in a warm cognac tone. Nine courses are $175 per person; wine pairing is an additional $95, though the by-the-glass list is strong enough that the pairing is optional rather than essential. Service is formal in training and warm in delivery. Reservations release 30 days in advance and fill quickly.
restate_1: finally got to Marigold Tempo after procrastinating for months because I told myself I was done with tasting menus in industrial spaces and I was wrong. Orsolya Fekete is doing something at that old printworks on Lasseter Row that doesn't fit a neat box: the techniques are modernist, the flavors are Central European in a way that's clearly personal. a compressed cucumber sphere in a cold broth of smoked bones and dried plum sounds like it's trying too hard and instead just tastes like the best version of something your grandmother might have made, except it's a sphere. nine courses, $175, wine pairing is $95 but ordering by the glass works great. the room is genuinely stunning: 14-meter ceilings, the old iron press still on the wall, banquettes in this warm cognac leather. the service team is formally trained but they're actually warm about it, which is rare. reservations open 30 days out, set a reminder, they go fast.
restate_2: Chef Orsolya Fekete has been quietly building one of the most interesting tasting menu programs in the city since Marigold Tempo opened in April in the old Lasseter Row printworks. Her sensibility resists categorization: the technique is modernist and precise, but the flavors are rooted in Central European family cooking, a combination that should create dissonance and instead produces coherence. Among the nine courses on the current $175 menu, a compressed cucumber sphere served in a cold smoked-bone and dried-plum broth stands out as a distillation of what Fekete is doing — unexpected in construction, recognizable in feeling. The room itself is worth the visit: 14-meter ceilings, the original iron press preserved against one wall, banquettes in a cognac-toned leather. Wine pairing is offered at $95 and the by-the-glass selection provides a reasonable alternative. The service team's warmth within a formally structured service is one of the more pleasant dynamics this city's dining scene currently offers. Reservations open on a 30-day rolling basis and are taken quickly.
restate_3: Marigold Tempo, Lasseter Row — nine courses, $175, wine pairing $95. You've probably already heard the name. Here is what I can add after going last week: Orsolya Fekete is doing something real at that old printworks. I went in slightly skeptical of the industrial-space tasting menu format and left reconsidering that skepticism. The cooking is modernist in its methods but the taste memory it triggers is Central European and personal — a compressed cucumber sphere in cold broth of smoked bones and dried plum reads as both avant-garde and like something that's been made in someone's kitchen for generations. 14-meter ceilings, the original iron press still bolted to the wall, cognac leather banquettes. We skipped the pairing and did by-the-glass and it worked. Service is trained but not stiff — genuinely warm. Go before you can't get a reservation.
restate_4: The old printworks on Lasseter Row has become home to Marigold Tempo, the April opening from Chef Orsolya Fekete that sits among the year's most significant restaurant debuts in the city. Fekete, whose cooking draws on the Central European flavors she grew up with while employing a rigorously modernist technique, has created a nine-course menu priced at $175 per person that defies straightforward categorization. A compressed cucumber sphere floating in a cold broth of smoked bones and dried plum — one of the meal's most striking courses — illustrates the approach: intellectually ambitious, emotionally resonant. The room, with its 14-meter ceilings, preserved iron printing press, and cognac-toned leather banquettes, is one of the more beautiful dining spaces to open this year. Wine pairing is available at $95 per person; by-the-glass service is also strong. The service team brings genuine warmth to a formally structured service. Reservations open 30 days in advance and fill within hours of release.

paragraph: I have been dreaming about the spicy yuba noodles at Lamplit since I had them three weeks ago and I cannot figure out why no one in my circle has been talking about this place. Lamplit is a 20-seat ramen-adjacent noodle shop on Hinden Avenue in the Quarry neighborhood, opened by chef Wren Solberg who apparently spent two years working noodle stations in various spots across a city she described in an interview only as very far away. the yuba noodles are silken tofu skin pulled into wide ribbons, dressed in a chili oil that has genuine complexity — you get the heat up front and then something almost floral, then a long finish of fermented black bean. the broth underneath is vegetable-based and cloudy and wonderful. full bowl is $16 and they do not do substitutions, which I respect. they also have a short list of small plates including a marinated soft egg with shiso for $4 that I got two of. the room is dim and warm, wooden stools at a bar plus four tables, open until midnight on weekends. I went on a Wednesday at 7pm and was seated immediately. go now before the line forms permanently.
restate_0: Lamplit, on Hinden Avenue in the Quarry neighborhood, is a 20-seat noodle shop that has been operating in relative obscurity despite producing food that deserves considerably more attention. Chef Wren Solberg, who speaks obliquely about the time she spent at noodle stations abroad before opening this place, has built a short menu around a signature dish that earns its central position: spicy yuba noodles at $16. Yuba — silken tofu skin — is pulled into wide ribbon shapes and dressed in a chili oil whose complexity unfolds in stages: initial heat, a floral middle, a long finish of fermented black bean. The broth is vegetable-based, cloudy, and deeply satisfying. No substitutions are offered or, based on the dish's internal coherence, needed. Small plates supplement the bowls, including a marinated soft egg with shiso at $4 that is worth ordering in multiples. The room is dim and intimate, wooden stools at a counter and four tables, open until midnight on weekends. A midweek visit yields immediate seating — a situation that seems unlikely to persist.
restate_1: three weeks out and I am still thinking about the spicy yuba noodles at Lamplit in the Quarry neighborhood. Wren Solberg runs this 20-seat noodle shop on Hinden Avenue and she spent two years working noodle stations somewhere she'll only describe as very far away and it shows. the yuba noodles ($16) are tofu skin pulled into wide ribbons and the chili oil is the thing — heat first, then something almost floral, then fermented black bean for days. vegetable broth underneath, cloudy and good. no substitutions and I understand and respect this. the marinated soft egg with shiso is $4 and I had two of them and I would have had three. dim room, wooden stools, midnight hours on weekends. I walked in at 7 on a Wednesday and sat down immediately, which will not be the case once more people find this place. go now.
restate_2: Lamplit doesn't advertise itself. The noodle shop — 20 seats on Hinden Avenue in the Quarry neighborhood — opened without fanfare, and Chef Wren Solberg, who spent two years at noodle stations in what she describes only as a city very far away, seems unbothered by the relative quiet. That quiet will not last once word reaches the people who care about this kind of cooking. The spicy yuba noodles ($16) are the thing to order: wide ribbons of silken tofu skin in a chili oil that delivers heat, then something floral, then a long fermented black bean finish, over a cloudy vegetable broth that is satisfying in its own right. The menu holds to no substitutions. A small plates section offers a marinated soft egg with shiso at $4. The space is dim and warm, open until midnight on weekends. Wednesday evening walk-ins are currently possible. This will change.
restate_3: Have you been to Lamplit yet? Because I'm genuinely confused why this place isn't more of a word-of-mouth freight train already. It's a 20-seat noodle shop on Hinden Avenue in the Quarry neighborhood, chef Wren Solberg at the helm, and she does one thing brilliantly: a spicy yuba noodle bowl for $16 that is the most interesting $16 bowl of anything I've eaten this year. Yuba pulled into wide ribbons, a chili oil with real layers (heat, then floral, then fermented black bean finish), and a cloudy vegetable broth underneath that ties everything together. No substitutions, which is the correct call. Also get the marinated soft egg with shiso ($4) — get two of them. The room is dark and warm and good, open till midnight on weekends. I went on a Wednesday at 7pm and walked right in. That window is closing.
restate_4: Nestled on Hinden Avenue in the Quarry neighborhood, Lamplit is a noodle shop that opened recently and has yet to attract the attention its food merits. Chef Wren Solberg, who developed her noodle craft over two years working abroad, runs a tight 20-seat operation with a focused menu anchored by the spicy yuba noodle bowl at $16. The preparation — tofu skin pulled into wide ribbons, dressed in a layered chili oil, set over a cloudy vegetable broth — is one of the more distinctive bowls in the city: the chili oil progresses from heat to floral to a fermented black bean finish in a way that rewards slow eating. No substitutions are available. A small plates list rounds out the menu, including a marinated soft egg with shiso ($4) that is the clear supporting player to order. The dining room is dim and comfortably spare — counter stools and four tables, open until midnight on weekends. Immediate seating is still possible on weeknights. Get there.

paragraph: so Pellara & Sons has been in the Fenwick Point neighborhood for about four months now and it is absolutely the kind of neighborhood Italian place I thought only existed in memories of places I'd been to once and never found again. this isn't fancy: red-checked tablecloths, a handwritten specials board, a wine list that's two pages and sensibly priced. the family behind it — Cosimo and his two adult kids, Giuliana and Raf — worked out of a catering operation for years before opening a permanent space. their cacio e pepe is exactly what cacio e pepe should be: nothing hidden, nothing extra, just good pasta and the quiet confidence of people who've made this thousands of times. the braised lamb with white beans and rosemary ($28) is genuinely one of the better things I've eaten in recent memory. they do a Tuesday pasta special where a bowl of whatever they feel like making is $14, and the room was packed for it when I stopped by. dessert is a rotating selection from a local bakery they've partnered with; I had a ricotta-filled pastry that was delicate and not too sweet. no reservations for parties under four.
restate_0: Pellara & Sons, which opened in the Fenwick Point neighborhood about four months ago, fills a gap that most diners don't realize they have until they walk in and feel its absence dissolve. Cosimo Pellara and his adult children, Giuliana and Raf, ran a catering operation for years before committing to a permanent space, and that background shows in the confidence of the food. This is not a restaurant chasing trends: red-checked tablecloths, a handwritten specials board, a two-page wine list at sensible prices. The cacio e pepe is the test, and it passes: no embellishments, no innovations, just properly made pasta with the calm assurance of a kitchen that has made this dish many hundreds of times. The braised lamb with white beans and rosemary, at $28, is one of the more satisfying plates in the neighborhood right now. The Tuesday pasta special — a bowl of whatever Cosimo feels like making, $14 — packed the room on a recent weeknight. Desserts rotate in from a local bakery partnership; a ricotta-filled pastry was delicate and just right. No reservations for parties under four.

restate_4: Pellara & Sons has operated in the Fenwick Point neighborhood for roughly four months, and in that time it has become the kind of neighborhood anchor that takes most restaurants years to establish. paragraph: the new Vantara pop-up running Thursday through Sunday evenings in the back of the Cordal Market building is worth knowing about even if the setup is chaotic. chef Priya Osei is cooking a loose interpretation of West African coastal flavors with some unexpected modernist flourishes — the charred plantain with smoked prawn butter and fermented pepper sauce ($14) sounds like it's doing too much and somehow doesn't. the menu changes weekly based on what she's sourcing, which means I can't promise what you'll find, but everything I had during my two visits was interesting and most of it was excellent. the space is genuinely a market backroom: folding tables, plastic chairs, a single overhead bulb per table. it's BYOB which helps keep costs down — the food itself runs $10-$18 per plate and you'd probably order three to four plates between two people. the crowd on a Friday was genuinely mixed, which I always take as a good sign. Osei is at the grill herself every service and will talk to you about the food if you ask; she's warm and precise about what she's doing and why. the pop-up is confirmed through the end of next month and I hope it finds a permanent home.

paragraph: Burnside Smoke & Ferment is the kind of restaurant that shouldn't work on paper — it's a barbecue and fermentation hybrid concept in a city not historically known for either — and yet here we are, six months after opening, and I'm already on my third visit. chef Tovah Krantz grew up in a family that kept a crock of something fermenting on the counter at all times, she says, and this informs everything. the smoked brisket is excellent on its own terms: 14-hour cook, properly rested, bark that crunches. but the move is to get it with the house kimchi made from green cabbage and smoked chili, and the fermented mustard that lives somewhere between German and Korean in its flavor logic. the sides lean heavily fermented too — sauerkraut with juniper and apple, pickled cucumbers with dill and miso. full plate with two sides is $26. the room is long and low, picnic-style seating, exposed brick, the smoker visible through a window at the back. no reservations, first come first served, they open at 11:30 and the line starts around 11. they sell out of brisket by 2pm most days, so plan accordingly.

paragraph: the new Solis outpost in the Greyvane shopping district is technically a fast-casual version of the original, which is a sentence that usually signals a decline in quality, but I have to say it mostly holds up. the original Solis on the west side has been one of my consistent favorites for their interpretations of Levantine flavors, and this newer smaller location — opened in March, designed for quick turnover with counter seating and a limited menu — manages to carry over the things that matter. the labneh and za'atar flatbread is still made to order and still excellent at $9. the chicken shawarma plate ($16) is juicy and well-spiced with pickled turnip and a garlic sauce that is legitimately very good. the lamb kofta wrap, however, is not quite the same as what the original does; the filling is dryer, the herbs less present. I mention it not as a dealbreaker but as information. the space fits maybe 25 people and moves quickly at lunch. they have a rotating seasonal drink that on my visit was a tamarind and dried rose number that was sweet but interesting. worth knowing about if you're in that part of town and hungry.

paragraph: Folded is a pasta bar that opened in the Rendell Square area in late fall and it is doing something that I think deserves more attention than it's gotten. the concept is simple: handmade pasta, roughly eight shapes on rotation, sauced simply, priced between $16 and $22. but the execution is where it gets interesting. Elara and Josip Buric, who run the kitchen together, have trained in pasta-making at a level that is apparent in the texture of every shape they serve — the pappardelle in brown butter with crispy sage and aged sheep's cheese ($18) had a chew that reminded me why handmade pasta is a different food category than dried pasta. the tajarin with truffle cream and black pepper ($22) was rich and quiet and exactly the right amount of food. the space is counter-and-window seating only, maybe 18 people total, warm lighting and soft music and the smell of butter in the air. they do lunch and dinner Wednesday through Saturday. reservations are taken via their website up to two weeks out; walk-ins are welcomed if seats are open. no desserts in-house but a gelato shop two doors down has an unofficial partnership with them, which is a nice detail.

paragraph: so the thing about Candle & Caraway is that it doesn't look like much from outside — a modest storefront on Bremer Street in the Westhollow neighborhood, a handwritten A-frame sign, curtains in the window — and then you walk in and it's this incredibly warm 28-seat room with mismatched antique furniture and bookshelves on every wall and food that is startlingly good. chef Anya Kroll is cooking what she describes as northern comfort food, which in practice means dishes built around preserved ingredients, long braises, and dairy in various forms. the smoked duck confit with celery root puree and pickled elderberry compote ($29) is the dish I've recommended to approximately nine people since I had it. the house bread comes with cultured butter and a small pot of fermented honey that I consumed an inappropriate amount of. dessert, a rye cake with lingonberry jam and cream, was $11 and excellent. the wine list is short and well-chosen and the by-the-glass options are reasonably priced for the neighborhood. service is warm and personal without being familiar in a way that makes you uncomfortable. I'm going back in two weeks. reservations recommended but they hold a few walk-in tables.
restate_0: Candle & Caraway, on Bremer Street in the Westhollow neighborhood, is the kind of place that conceals its quality behind an understated exterior — a handwritten A-frame, curtains in the window, a modest storefront — and then makes you feel like you found something when you step inside. The 28-seat room is warm and deliberately assembled: mismatched antique furniture, bookshelves on every wall, the kind of coziness that takes thought to achieve without becoming affected. Chef Anya Kroll's northern comfort food approach means preserved ingredients, long braises, dairy in various forms. The smoked duck confit with celery root puree and pickled elderberry compote ($29) is the standout — a dish that has been passed along across nine separate recommendations since a recent visit. paragraph: the most exciting thing to open in the Saltmere neighborhood in recent memory is probably Braise & Botanical, a 40-seat spot run by chef Cullen Ashford who came up through fine dining and has clearly made a deliberate decision to cook for people rather than for awards. the menu is seasonal and changes frequently but the pillars are there: something braised, something from the wood grill, a composed salad that earns its $14 price tag, and a dessert situation that leans toward warm and comforting rather than architectural. on my visit the short rib was braised with stout and molasses and served with potato gratin and gremolata ($34), and it was the sort of dish that makes you go quiet for a moment when you take the first bite. the botanical half of the name refers to a small cocktail program that uses foraged and house-dried herbs in a way that is interesting without being gimmicky — the smoked thyme old fashioned ($16) was genuinely one of the better cocktails I've had in a restaurant setting. the room has the warm, lived-in feeling of a place that's been there for twenty years even though it opened in March. I think it'll actually last that long.

paragraph: Whetstone Grill has been open in the Carnel district for about six weeks and I have complicated feelings about it. let me explain. the food is very good — chef Dov Steinmann is a technically accomplished cook and the wood-grilled lamb chops ($38) are among the better lamb I've had this year, with a crust that is properly charred and a center that is rosy and yielding. the grilled vegetables are handled with real care, not an afterthought. the desserts are all house-made and the olive oil cake ($10) is simple and lovely. but the service has some kinks: our water glasses went empty twice, a dish arrived before the previous course was cleared, and one member of our party sat for 20 minutes before anyone came to explain the specials. none of this would be worth mentioning for a place in its second week, but at $90+ per person for the kind of experience being sold, the expectation is higher. I suspect these are opening-month issues that will smooth out; the bones are good and the kitchen is clearly talented. I'd go back in a few months and I think the assessment would be different. worth watching.

paragraph: if you are the kind of person who has strong feelings about ramen broth and you haven't been to Torchwood yet I genuinely do not know what you are waiting for. paragraph: there's a Korean-Mexican hybrid situation that's been running out of a food truck in the Norgate warehouse district on weekends and I want to document it before it disappears or, ideally, before it gets too famous to approach on foot. the truck is called Gochujang & Lime and it's operated by two people, Seo-yeon Park and her partner Mateo Rios, who are doing something that sounds gimmicky and actually isn't. the bulgogi taco ($5 each, two minimum) uses a tortilla that's corn-based and pressed to order, and the bulgogi itself is properly marinated and hits the grill right before it goes in the wrap. the topping situation — house kimchi, crema cut with gochujang, pickled white onion, lime — is balanced in a way that's clearly been thought about. they also do a black bean and miso soup ($6) that is the kind of thing that makes you think about the overlap between Korean and Mexican flavor logic in a new way. the truck runs Saturday and Sunday from 11am to sellout, usually 3 or 4pm. no card, cash only, there's an ATM in the coffee shop 50 feet away. the line moves fast. this is exactly the kind of thing that should become a restaurant.

paragraph: I went to Clove & Copper for my birthday dinner and I'm now deeply regretting not going sooner because apparently this place has been open on Merchant Row in the Aldwell district for eight months and I just didn't get around to it. chef Lian Soo is doing a personal interpretation of Southeast Asian flavors that doesn't collapse into a single country's cuisine — it borrows from everywhere and synthesizes into something that feels like her own voice. the standout dish, and I will fight about this, is the Hainanese chicken rice reinterpreted as a composed plate: silky poached chicken over fragrant rice cooked in chicken stock, with a deeply savory dark soy, a ginger scallion sauce, and a clear broth served alongside. $26 and genuinely one of the most comforting things I've eaten in a long time. the pandan custard dessert ($9) is set with a slightly looser wobble than you'd expect and served with coconut caramel, which is the right call. the room is calm and clean, dark wood and white linen without being stuffy about it. the wine list has some thoughtful natural options. service was professional and warm. I'm going back next month.
restate_0: Clove & Copper has been open on Merchant Row in the Aldwell district for eight months, and anyone who has been sleeping on it — as I was until a birthday dinner this week — should correct that immediately. Chef Lian Soo is working across Southeast Asian culinary traditions without reducing the result to any single one, and the food carries the specificity of genuine synthesis rather than the vagueness of fusion. The signature dish — a composed interpretation of Hainanese chicken rice — delivers silky poached chicken over fragrant chicken-stock rice with dark soy, ginger scallion sauce, and a clear broth served alongside, all for $26. It is one of the more comforting plates this city currently offers. The pandan custard ($9), slightly looser than expected and served with coconut caramel, is the dessert to get. The room is clean and quiet — dark wood and white linen — and the natural wine list has been thoughtfully assembled. Service is professional and warm. Worth eight months' regret for finally going; worth going again next month.

paragraph: here is an opening that snuck up on me: Dune & Salt is a small seafood-focused restaurant in the Seavale neighborhood that opened in early March and which I only found out about because a friend texted me a photo of a plate of clams that looked so good I asked where she was immediately. the restaurant is doing what they call tide-to-table, which means the menu is organized around whatever was caught or harvested locally in the past 48 hours, and it changes accordingly. on my visit the menu had grilled razor clams with brown butter and capers ($16), a whole roasted sea bass with fennel and preserved lemon ($38, serves two comfortably), and a chowder that was thick and creamy with chunks of what was identified as day-boat cod and local potatoes ($14). all of it was excellent. the grilled clams in particular were briny and sweet and the capers cut through the richness perfectly. the space is small — 22 seats — and has the blue-and-white palette you'd expect, but it's restrained and not nautical-themed in an embarrassing way. reservations via their website, open Wednesday through Sunday, dinner only.

Grilled razor clams with brown butter and capers ($16) were briny and sweet with the capers cutting through the richness at precisely the right moment. The 22-seat room avoids the nautical-kitsch trap through restraint: blue-and-white palette, nothing embarrassing on the walls. Open Wednesday through Sunday for dinner, reservations through the website. One of the better things to open in this neighborhood in years.

paragraph: the thing about Gather & Ferment that I can't fully explain is how much I want to go back even though I didn't love everything I ate there. it opened last spring in the Calloway arts district in a repurposed soap factory, and the space is extraordinary — double-height ceilings, the original mixing vats painted and illuminated and incorporated into the decor, long communal tables of reclaimed elm. the chef, Rowan Birch, is fermentation-obsessed in a way that produces uneven results: some dishes are elevated by the fermented components (the aged white soy on the grilled eggplant was a revelation) and some are overwhelmed by them (the fermented black garlic sauce on the lamb chop just made everything taste the same). but I left thinking about the meal in a way I don't with most perfectly executed but safe food. the natural wine list is one of the better I've seen in this city. the service is enthusiastic and knowledgeable. the five-course menu is $95 per person. I think I'm going back to try the other side of the menu and report back.
restate_0: Gather & Ferment, now several months into its run in a repurposed soap factory in the Calloway arts district, is the kind of restaurant that stays with you despite its inconsistencies — which is, arguably, the more interesting problem to have. Chef Rowan Birch's fermentation obsession is the defining feature of a five-course, $95-per-person menu that achieves genuine elevation in some courses and redundancy in others. The aged white soy on the grilled eggplant is a revelation: the fermentation lifts and focuses rather than dominates. The fermented black garlic sauce on the lamb chop does the opposite — everything resolves to the same note, and the lamb loses its own character. But the meal generates thought in proportion to its missteps, which is not true of most correctly executed menus. The space — double-height ceilings, original soap vats incorporated into the decor, reclaimed elm communal tables — is one of the more remarkable dining rooms to open this year. The natural wine list is excellent. Service is enthusiastic and well-informed. Worth returning to.

paragraph: let me tell you about the best $12 lunch I've had in recent memory, which is a statement I don't make lightly. Yalda Kitchen is a counter-service spot in the Brimswick market district run by a woman named Farideh who I was told has been feeding people in this building in one form or another for fifteen years, though the current iteration under the name Yalda opened only last fall. the format is simple: two mains per day, served with rice and salad and a small pot of yogurt with herbs. on my visit it was ghormeh sabzi — a deeply fragrant herb and kidney bean stew with lamb — and a roasted cauliflower and chickpea dish fragrant with turmeric and dried lime. the ghormeh sabzi was exactly as good as ghormeh sabzi can be, which is a thing I say knowing that ghormeh sabzi done right is very good indeed. the yogurt was house-made and better than most restaurant yogurt I've had. Farideh moves quickly and with total confidence and the line at noon is 15 people deep but it moves fast. cash preferred. I went back the next day.

paragraph: Crestfall Dining Room is the new tasting menu restaurant in the Orvale hotel, which opened its doors to the public last month, and I went in with lowish expectations because hotel restaurants that are meant to be serious destination dining are often more serious in concept than in execution. chef Vera Lindqvist proved me wrong. the menu is eight courses at $165 per person and it takes you through what Lindqvist describes as a personal geography of Nordic and Japanese flavor logic, which is a description that could go wrong in many ways and in her hands does not. a dashi made from kombu and smoked reindeer, served in a vessel that looks like a flat stone, is quiet and perfect. a course of thinly sliced pickled root vegetables with a miso and walnut cream manages to be both delicate and grounding. the technical precision is evident but never showy. the hotel context means the room is larger and better lit than a standalone restaurant of this ambition might be — 40 seats, which is a lot for a tasting menu — but the service team handles it with unusual grace. this is one of the restaurants I'm going to tell people about for a while.
restate_0: The Crestfall Dining Room, the tasting menu restaurant inside the Orvale hotel, opened last month with the kind of concept description — Nordic and Japanese flavor synthesis — that generates skepticism before the first course. Chef Vera Lindqvist dispatches that skepticism methodically across eight courses at $165 per person. A dashi made from kombu and smoked reindeer, served in a stone-like vessel, is quiet and exact in a way that instantly signals that the Japanese-Nordic framing has been genuinely internalized rather than borrowed as aesthetic. Thinly sliced pickled root vegetables with miso and walnut cream is a course that manages delicacy and groundedness simultaneously. The technical precision throughout is evident and never self-congratulatory. The hotel context means 40 seats, which is a generous footprint for this level of ambition, but the service team navigates it with notable grace. This is the rare hotel restaurant that earns its serious reputation through the food.

A course of pickled root vegetables, thinly sliced, with miso and walnut cream is both precise and comforting. Lindqvist's technical skill is present throughout without drawing attention to itself. The hotel dining room is larger than a standalone tasting menu restaurant of this ambition would typically be — 40 seats — but the service team manages the scale with unusual poise. The Crestfall Dining Room is the compelling argument that hotel restaurants can execute at the level of the city's best standalone tasting menus.

restate_4: The Crestfall Dining Room opened last month inside the Orvale hotel, offering an eight-course tasting menu at $165 per person under the direction of Chef Vera Lindqvist. paragraph: I have been going to the Sable & Thyme night market every Friday since it launched in May and I want to write about it properly because it's the kind of thing that deserves documentation before it either becomes permanently established or disappears. it runs from 6pm to midnight along the lower end of Cavendish Walk in the Lorne district — about 30 vendors, mostly food, a few craft stalls, one excellent cocktail bar that appears from nowhere at dusk. the food vendor I keep returning to is a stall called Roof Garden that does herb-forward small plates — a lamb tartare with preserved lemon and herb oil ($11), grilled halloumi with pomegranate and za'atar honey ($9), a rotating flatbread with whatever seasonal topping the cook feels like ($7). there's also a dumplings stall, Fold & Press, whose pork and chive dumplings ($10 for eight) are boiled and then pan-seared to order and are excellent. the market has a relaxed, neighborhood feel without being precious about it. bring cash because several vendors don't take cards, though a couple do. this is genuinely one of the things I look forward to most about warmer weather.
restate_0: The Sable & Thyme night market, which launched in May along the lower end of Cavendish Walk in the Lorne district, runs every Friday from 6pm to midnight and has quickly become one of the neighborhood's most anticipated weekly events. About 30 vendors — mostly food, with a few craft stalls and one cocktail bar that materializes at dusk — occupy the walk in a relaxed and unpretentious arrangement. Among the food vendors, the Roof Garden stall is the standout: herb-forward small plates including lamb tartare with preserved lemon and herb oil ($11), grilled halloumi with pomegranate and za'atar honey ($9), and a rotating seasonal flatbread at $7. The dumpling stall Fold & Press offers pork and chive dumplings ($10 for eight) that are boiled and then pan-seared to order, a two-step process that produces a better dumpling than either method alone. Cash is strongly recommended, as several vendors do not process cards. A reliable warm-weather Friday destination.

paragraph: so the Oaken Plate did a soft open last weekend and I happened to be in the Marren neighborhood so I walked in, expecting to be turned away, and instead ended up eating one of the more quietly impressive dinners I've had in recent months. the restaurant is in a nineteenth-century house that's been thoughtfully renovated — original plaster details, waxed wood floors, a sort of hushed domestic quality that makes you want to speak in lower tones. chef Pawel Grodnicki is cooking what I'd call considered European: not fusion, not any single tradition, but rather a point of view that takes classical technique and applies it to locally sourced ingredients without making a fuss about either. the duck breast rested on a bed of braised Savoy cabbage with a cherry and juniper jus ($32) was the kind of dish that rewards attention — not obviously spectacular but deeply satisfying in the way of something very well made. the cheese course ($18) had five selections with excellent accompaniments. wine list skews French and Austrian. they're doing reservations starting next week; I'd get in early while the buzz is still building.
restate_0: The Oaken Plate, which soft-opened in the Marren neighborhood last weekend in a renovated nineteenth-century house, is producing food that suggests one of the more quietly accomplished debuts of the season. The dining room — original plaster work, waxed wood floors, a hushed domestic scale — creates an atmosphere that suits the cooking: considered and undemonstrative. Chef Pawel Grodnicki is working in what might be called classical European with a personal point of view — technique from the tradition, ingredients sourced locally, no cuisine flag planted in the name. The duck breast over braised Savoy cabbage with cherry and juniper jus ($32) is the kind of dish that reveals its quality over the course of eating rather than on first appearance: deeply satisfying, very well made. The five-selection cheese course at $18 is excellent in both content and accompaniments. The wine list skews French and Austrian. Reservations open next week; the recommendation is to book early.

paragraph: can we talk about how good Sunken Garden is? because I feel like not enough people in my circle are talking about Sunken Garden. this is an Indian-inspired tasting menu restaurant in the Delmore Row neighborhood that opened in February, run by chef Amara Krishnaswami who trained in a traditional south Indian household and then spent years working in modernist European kitchens and has now combined these influences into a menu that is genuinely its own thing. the meal starts with a single spiced cashew on a small leaf — a gesture that is humble and confident simultaneously — and builds to a 10-course progression at $145 per person that includes things like a compressed watermelon with tamarind gel and puffed amaranth, a slow-cooked lamb with 24 spices finished with a tableside pour of a warm coconut broth, and a dessert of cardamom ice cream melting into a pool of saffron caramel. the room has 16 seats, a garden visible through the rear window, quiet lighting. the service is knowledgeable and not stuffy. reservations open on the first of each month for the following month; they go quickly.

From there: compressed watermelon with tamarind gel and puffed amaranth; slow-cooked lamb with 24 spices, finished at the table with a pour of warm coconut broth; cardamom ice cream melting into saffron caramel. paragraph: the new Torben & Lark bar and small plates place on Wicklow Lane in the Fernshaw district is giving me something I didn't know I needed: a genuinely good late-night food option that isn't pizza or a sad cheese plate. they opened in April with a menu that's clearly designed for the 10pm-and-later crowd — things you want after a show or a few drinks, but made with real care. the deviled eggs with smoked trout mousse ($8 for three) are a beautiful thing. the roasted bone marrow with herb salad and sourdough ($16) is exactly as good as bone marrow should be, which is very good. there's a short burger section that I initially dismissed and then had on a friend's recommendation: the smash burger with caramelized onions and a house sauce on a potato roll ($14) is not trying to be fancy and is the better for it. the bar program is serious — an excellent amaro list, thoughtful cocktails, a beer selection that has clearly been considered. the room is dark and convivial and the music is loud but not deafening. open until 2am Friday and Saturday. this is exactly the gap this neighborhood needed filled.

paragraph: Verdant opened on the second floor of the Ellison building in the Gresham district in early spring and it is the most ambitious vegan fine dining restaurant I've been to in this city, which is a category that has historically ranged from sincere and mediocre to performative and expensive. Verdant is neither of those things. chef Simone Aldric is cooking with a precision and an imagination that would be notable in any context, and the vegetables and plants she's working with are treated as the main event rather than the workaround. a celery root aged for four weeks and served with black truffle oil and a powder of dried mushroom might sound like it's working very hard, and it is, but it earns the effort. a course of heritage grains — freekeh, emmer, and a grain I didn't recognize — served with cultured nut cream and herb oil was genuinely moving in a way I didn't anticipate. the 10-course menu is $155 per person. the wine and drink pairing includes natural wines and a non-alcoholic botanical pairing option ($55 each), both of which are thoughtfully designed. this is the kind of restaurant that makes you reconsider where the ceiling is.
restate_0: Verdant, which opened in early spring on the second floor of the Ellison building in the Gresham district, has set a new standard for vegan fine dining in this city — a category that has rarely produced food worth this much attention. Chef Simone Aldric's 10-course menu at $155 per person treats plant and vegetable ingredients as the primary material of serious cooking, and the execution demonstrates that the premise is not a constraint but a focus. A celery root aged for four weeks, served with black truffle oil and dried mushroom powder, does a considerable amount of work and earns every bit of it. A heritage grain course — freekeh, emmer, and an unnamed third — served with cultured nut cream and herb oil is the kind of dish that produces genuine feeling rather than simply intellectual appreciation. A wine and non-alcoholic botanical pairing, both $55, are thoughtfully designed. This is the restaurant that resets expectations for what plant-based fine dining can achieve.

restate_4: Verdant, which opened on the second floor of the Ellison building in the Gresham district in early spring, has established itself as the most technically accomplished vegan fine dining restaurant in the city. The results support the premise: a four-week-aged celery root with black truffle oil and dried mushroom powder is a course that works hard and delivers; a heritage grain course of freekeh, emmer, and a third variety with cultured nut cream and herb oil is among the more genuinely affecting dishes the city offers this season. paragraph: I went to the opening weekend of Sotto Voce and it was crowded in the way that makes you wonder if you're going to be able to properly evaluate the food, but I think I can say with confidence that the food is very good and the room is beautiful and they are going to be fine once the chaos of opening week settles down. the restaurant is in the basement of the Garlan building on Pont Street in the old downtown, and the conceit — a subterranean Italian-inspired spot that feels like it's been there for decades — is executed well. exposed brick, small tables close together, candles in wine bottles, a short menu that changes weekly. the pasta is made in-house and the tagliatelle with wild boar ragu ($22) was rich and long-cooked and exactly what you want in a basement room in the middle of a cold spring evening. the tiramisu ($10) was properly soaked and not too sweet. service was stretched thin but never unfriendly. the wine list leans Italian, sensibly priced, with some interesting southern Italian choices I hadn't encountered before. dinner only, Tuesday through Sunday, reservations required.
restate_0: Sotto Voce opened its doors this past weekend in the basement of the Garlan building on Pont Street in old downtown, and an opening weekend visit is not the ideal condition for evaluation — but what was visible through the chaos is promising. The concept is well-executed: a subterranean Italian-inspired restaurant designed to feel like it has existed for decades, with exposed brick, wine-bottle candles, and small tables close together. The pasta is made in-house, and the tagliatelle with wild boar ragu ($22) is the kind of long-cooked, rich main that earns its context in a dark basement room on a cold spring night. The tiramisu at $10 is properly soaked and not oversweetened. Service was stretched under opening weekend pressure but remained consistently pleasant. paragraph: the thing about Nettlewood is that it's been open for four months now in the Southfen neighborhood and it still doesn't have a sign outside, which is either an oversight or a choice, and based on the food I think it's a choice. chef Cassian Fell is doing the kind of hyper-local, ingredient-first cooking that benefits from a sense of restraint in the marketing because the room — 18 seats, linen napkins, no music — lets the food speak without competition. I went on a Tuesday and had seven courses for $130 per person. highlights: a thin tart of caramelized onion and aged cheese with a thyme crust that was precisely $11 worth of happiness; a whole roasted beet with herb oil and a sauce made from beet tops that was simple to the point of austerity and somehow also one of the best things I've eaten in the last six months; a milk chocolate and salted caramel dessert that was generous and not overthought. Fell comes out after service to talk about the menu and he's interesting and unpretentious about it. no website, booking by phone only, open Thursday through Sunday. this is exactly the kind of place I want to exist.

paragraph: Okafor's opened in the Birchton neighborhood in January and I want to be careful about how I describe it because I think the easy framing — West African restaurant opening — undersells what's actually being done here. chef Emeka Okafor grew up moving between three countries and has eaten and cooked across a wide geography, and what he's building at Okafor's is a menu that draws on the full range of that experience without being constrained by it. the suya-spiced lamb ribs ($26) are the dish everyone orders and they deserve the attention: the spice blend is complex, the meat is properly fatty and tender, the pickled onion and pepper alongside is perfect. but the egusi soup with pounded yam ($19), which you might miss because it's lower on the menu, is extraordinary — deeply layered, fermented in ways that take time to parse. the room is warm and richly decorated, prints on the walls, a low honey-colored ceiling, good music playing at a volume that allows conversation. service is knowledgeable and enthusiastic without being overwhelming. book ahead, especially Friday and Saturday.

paragraph: the Helm debuted in a shipping container cluster development called the Kestrel Yards in the far Dorwick neighborhood in late March, and yes, I know what you're thinking because I thought it too. container development restaurants are a mixed bag at best. but the Helm is legitimately good and I say this having been three times. it's a small plates and natural wine concept run by Bex and Jonno Tarrant, who are doing Antipodean-inflected cooking — bright, acidic, herb-forward, not shy about fermented things. the kingfish crudo with yuzu dressing and crispy capers ($16) is the first thing I'd order every time. the pork belly with green papaya salad and a sweet-tart tamarind dressing ($22) was a very happy surprise. the smashed cucumber salad ($9) is $9 well spent. the wine list is fun and the staff know it well and are enthusiastic about helping you navigate it. the container setting means the space gets loud when full — about 40 covers — and the heating situation in cooler weather is imperfect. but the food makes it worth it, and the outdoor seating area, when the weather cooperates, is genuinely lovely.

paragraph: I want to document Spice & Tempo properly before it gets too crowded to enjoy, which I suspect will happen. this is a 24-seat Sri Lankan restaurant in the Coppice Lane neighborhood that opened in February, run by a family — mother Kamala, daughter Pritha, and son Anand — who have been selling short eats from a market stall in the same area for six years and finally moved into a permanent space. the kottu roti ($16) is the dish I keep coming back to: shredded flatbread stir-fried on an iron griddle with egg, vegetables, and your choice of protein, the whole thing smoky and fragrant with a spice blend that has serious depth. the fish curry ($18) is a tamarind-forward, coconut milk-based thing that is rich and not excessively hot, nuanced in a way that rewards attention. the pol sambol — a fresh coconut relish with chili and lime that comes alongside most dishes — is one of those condiments that you eat by itself and then are sorry it's gone. the room is bright and simply decorated, long table in the center, smaller tables at the sides. service is from the family, warm and unhurried. cash strongly preferred.

paragraph: there is a nine-seat omakase counter called Briar that opened in the Velt neighborhood last month and I have already decided it is one of my ten favorite restaurants in the city, which is not a ranking I update lightly. chef Ros Maddox is doing a hyperlocal produce-driven menu with 10 courses at $160 per person, no wine pairing available but a curated list of bottles you can order from. the sensibility is quietly modernist in technique but the flavors are warm and not austere — this is food that makes you comfortable even as it surprises you. a course of spring onion cooked directly in coals, peeled at the table, and served with a cultured cream and herb oil had a smokiness that was precise rather than aggressive. a small bowl of grains in brown butter with a shaving of aged cheese was the kind of quiet pleasure that you keep coming back to mentally for days afterward. the counter setting means you're watching Maddox and her one assistant cook everything in real time, which is its own kind of experience. reservations open the 15th of each month for the following month. I set a reminder.

paragraph: Coppice Kitchen does one thing and does it exceptionally: wood-fired, fermented, and cured things in various combinations, in a small room on Orchard Way in the Belsten district that opened last November. chef Tomas Krahl has set up a larder-forward kitchen where the base of almost everything is something he made two weeks or two months ago — a cured pork loin, a fermented vegetable paste, a fruit vinegar in progress. the result is food with an uncommon depth of flavor. the charcuterie plate ($24 for four items) is the anchor of the menu and a reason to come by itself: the house-cured bresaola has the kind of clean, concentrated flavor you get from someone who's spent real time learning how to do this. the smoked duck leg with pickled red cabbage and caraway ($28) is a complete dish that doesn't need anything else. bread is made daily, a rough levain that is crusty and chewy and served with cultured butter and a small dish of lard. the room is 20 seats, stone walls, a fireplace that runs in winter. dinner Thursday through Saturday, lunch Friday and Saturday only. worth the trip to Belsten.

paragraph: so Lorimer's has been the talk of the Arden neighborhood for the last two months and I finally got in on a Wednesday and my overall verdict is that the hype is justified with minor caveats. chef and owner Margot Schell, who worked in a number of well-regarded kitchens before opening this place in mid-February, is cooking a menu she describes as instinctive seasonal — which is to say, she's not following a cuisine tradition so much as following a philosophy of using what's best and cooking it in ways that feel right for the season. the current standout is a whole roasted trout stuffed with fennel frond and lemon, served with roasted almonds and a butter-herb sauce ($32), which is a dish that sounds like something but on the plate reveals itself to be done with an unusual lightness of touch. the braised leeks with aged cheese and breadcrumbs ($14) are among the better things I've had to start a meal this year. the caveats: the desserts on my visit were good but not at the level of the savory courses, and the acoustics make conversation difficult when the room is full. but the food is doing enough that I'd go back soon.

paragraph: okay I need to write about Stormcloud Bao before more people find out about it because the bao situation is already precarious. paragraph: the new Kessler & Vine is nominally a wine bar but the food is serious enough that calling it a wine bar undersells what it is. it opened on Millgate Road in the Osterley district in early April, run by sommelier Petra Vance and chef Willard Soo, who have built a menu designed around wine pairing in a way that doesn't mean small portions of dainty things. the food is generous and thoughtful: a plate of house-cured anchovies with butter and radishes ($12) is the right start, and from there you can build a meal from the small plates section — the ricotta gnudi with brown butter and sage ($16) are pillowy and rich, the duck rillette with cornichons and mustard ($18) is a proper portion — or move to larger format dishes like the whole roasted chicken for two ($48) which is worth every dollar. the wine list runs to 150 selections and skews European with some interesting outliers; the by-the-glass selection is about 20 options and changes regularly. the room is warm and slightly intimate — 35 seats, low light, a marble bar at the center. I've been for both an early weeknight dinner and a late Saturday drink and it works at both times.

paragraph: there's a new dim sum restaurant in the Radcliffe neighborhood called Plum Hall that opened in March and which is quickly becoming one of my most reliable weekend-morning reasons to leave the house. the space is large — 80 seats — and operates on a traditional cart system, which you don't see often and which I find deeply satisfying as a format: the carts come to you, you point at what you want, you eat it while it's hot. the har gow ($6 for three) are properly translucent, the filling tightly packed and fresh-tasting. the char siu bao ($6 for three, steamed) are fluffy and the filling is sweet and savory in the right proportions. the turnip cake, pan-fried to order at the cart, is $5 and among the better I've had — properly rice-flour dense, crispy outside, and the soy and chili dipping situation is right. the room is loud, family-oriented, and completely without pretension. tea service is $2 per person and the chrysanthemum tea is excellent. service on the carts is brisk and helpful. open from 9:30am to 3pm on weekends only. this is exactly the dim sum experience this neighborhood has been missing.

paragraph: I want to close out this run of restaurant thoughts with Ember Rye, which opened last month in the Sandhollow district in a space that used to be a post office, and which has managed to build a sensibility I find genuinely hard to categorize, which is rare. chef duo Isla and Felix Strand are doing what they call Scandinavian-Irish cooking, a combination that sounds like a joke but in practice produces food that makes a lot of internal sense — both traditions share a love of preserved things, dairy, root vegetables, and the sea, and the Strands have worked out a menu that brings these together without irony. the smoked mackerel with soda bread, cultured cream, and pickled dill cucumber ($14) was a beautiful opener. the braised beef cheek with celeriac gratin and a rye breadcrumb gremolata ($32) was the kind of main that justifies the journey. the dessert menu has an aquavit-poached pear with brown butter cake ($11) that is the dessert I would want if I could only have one. the space retains some postal details — the sorting counter is now the bar, the original mailboxes line one wall — and it's a very good room. reservations by phone or email. I'm going back next month.
